Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set aside blocks of time during the week, maybe 15 hours total, but it rarely went according to plan. I'd often end up studying late at night when things got quiet, reading through materials and taking notes in my notebook. With everything going on in my life and limited resources, I had to be flexible and grab study time whenever I could find it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in regularly and keeping up with content. But other weeks, especially when I was dealing with stress or anxiety, I'd fall behind and then have to cram everything near deadlines. I tried to create regularity but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about new content that would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed, especially since my attendance wasn't great and I worried about falling behind. The anxiety of potentially missing something important was often what pushed me to check the LMS, even when I didn't feel like it.
Q4: Yeah, there were periods where logging in became more automatic for me. I think it happened when I got into a rhythm with the discussion posts and started engaging with other students. Having those 14 posts to contribute made me feel like I was part of something, and checking in became a habit. The mindfulness exercises I started doing also helped me approach the LMS with less dread.
Q5: The reading materials and discussion forums were most useful to me. I'm a read-write learner, so being able to go through text-based content at my own pace really worked for my brain. The discussions were helpful too because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. I'd often copy key points into my own notes and study from those later.
Q6: I'll be honest, I skipped a lot of the video content or only partially watched it. With over 165 minutes of video, I found myself getting bored and distracted. Videos require good internet and uninterrupted time, and with my limited resources, that wasn't always possible. I'd often just skim through looking for key points rather than watching the whole thing.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me complete something that counted toward my grade, I was more invested. Also, activities that I could engage with on my own terms, like reading materials I could download and review offline, felt more valuable because they fit my situation better.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford. When you're stressed and anxious like I am, and you have limited time and resources, you have to prioritize what's required. Required activities had clear consequences if I didn't do them, so those always came first. Optional stuff only happened if I had a rare moment of extra energy or curiosity.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study them on my phone during commutes or when my internet was unreliable. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd write out key concepts and create my own summaries. Sometimes I'd find free resources online that explained things better than the course materials.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course content but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd print out readings and annotate them by hand, or when I'd spend hours working through problems in my notebook. I also had study sessions with a couple of classmates in person where we'd discuss concepts, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work through them offline over several days. My internet access isn't always reliable, and honestly it saved data costs too. So I might show one login and one download, but that material got studied multiple times over the following week.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety was really high. I'd feel overwhelmed looking at all the modules and assignments, and sometimes I'd just shut down and avoid the LMS entirely. The boredom would hit hard too, making it difficult to push through content that didn't grab my attention. Those were the weeks my attendance really suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with financial stress, the LMS became just another source of anxiety. I'd log in with good intentions but find myself unable to focus, just clicking through things without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and other essential things, and studying didn't always win.
Q14: Yeah, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through content, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about bills, or feeling anxious about upcoming deadlines, and the words on the screen just wouldn't stick. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though technically I was 'engaged' with the platform.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially longer assignments or content that seemed boring or confusing. My anxiety would make me avoid things that felt overwhelming, even when I knew procrastinating would make it worse. It's like a cycle where the more important something is, the more anxious I get, and the more I avoid it. I'm working on this but it's hard.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator, for better or worse. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage intensively with the LMS. My activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before things were due. It's not ideal and it added to my stress, but it was often the only way I could push through the boredom and anxiety to actually complete things.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me more likely to engage with similar activities. But critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid looking at grades for days. When I finally started the mindfulness practices, I got better at handling feedback without spiraling, which helped my engagement overall.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty helpful because it broke things into manageable chunks. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap even when I felt lost. But sometimes the structure felt rigid and didn't account for weeks when life got complicated. The 19 online courses I was juggling made it hard to give each one the attention the structure demanded.
Q19: The text-based resources made engagement easier for me since that matches how I learn best. The discussion forums were also well-designed and helped me feel connected to classmates. What made it harder was when content was only available in video format or required constant internet access. Also, some modules felt unnecessarily long, which fed into my boredom.
Q20: I can totally see how that happens. Sometimes you're searching for something specific, clicking around trying to understand what's expected, or just anxiously checking things without actually producing work. High activity might also mean someone is struggling to understand the material and keeps revisiting it. Activity doesn't equal productivity, especially when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my situation in some ways. I performed reasonably well despite inconsistent LMS activity because I did so much studying offline. If you have good strategies for learning outside the platform and can perform when it counts, your grades won't necessarily reflect your login frequency. The LMS is just one tool, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accessed materials, and patterns around deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the mental state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone was deeply focused or anxiously scrolling. They also miss collaborative learning that happens outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just going through the motions. It's when the material makes sense and you want to understand more, when you're thinking about concepts even outside of study time. For me, engagement is also about pushing through the boredom and anxiety to find something meaningful in the content.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I've had to fight to stay in this course despite limited resources and high anxiety. It can't capture the mindfulness exercises that helped me manage my stress, or the hours I spent with downloaded materials when my internet was down. It doesn't show my resilience in climbing back after rough weeks, or the genuine learning that happened in my notebook and in conversations with peers.
